steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries question. The sentence contains two independent clauses joined by the conjunctive adverb "on the contrary." The SAT rule for conjunctive adverbs connecting two independent clauses is: semicolon before, comma after (IC; conjunctive adverb, IC).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'rare, on the contrary,': A comma alone cannot join two independent clauses; this creates a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — 'rare, on the contrary;': The comma is before the conjunctive adverb and the semicolon is after it, which is the reverse of the required pattern and leaves the first clause improperly joined. ✗
  • Choice C — 'rare on the contrary,': No punctuation separates the two independent clauses at all, creating a fused sentence. ✗
  • Choice D — 'rare; on the contrary,': The semicolon comes before "on the contrary" and the comma follows it, correctly applying the IC; conjunctive adverb, IC pattern to join two independent clauses. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
